findings.title: Hardcoded password detected
findings.description: A password appears to be hardcoded in the source code. This is a severe data security risk as it can be extracted from version control, build artifacts, or decompiled binaries.
findings.recommendation: Move the password to a secrets manager (e.g., Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) or at minimum to environment variables. Never commit secrets to source control.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace hardcoded password with an environment variable: process.env.SECRET_NAME or inject from a secrets manager at runtime.

findings.description: A API key appears to be hardcoded in the source code. This is a severe data security risk as it can be extracted from version control, build artifacts, or decompiled binaries.
findings.recommendation: Move the API key to a secrets manager (e.g., Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) or at minimum to environment variables. Never commit secrets to source control.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace hardcoded API key with an environment variable: process.env.SECRET_NAME or inject from a secrets manager at runtime.

findings.title: Hardcoded secret/token detected
findings.description: A secret/token appears to be hardcoded in the source code. This is a severe data security risk as it can be extracted from version control, build artifacts, or decompiled binaries.
findings.recommendation: Move the secret/token to a secrets manager (e.g., Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) or at minimum to environment variables. Never commit secrets to source control.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace hardcoded secret/token with an environment variable: process.env.SECRET_NAME or inject from a secrets manager at runtime.

findings.title: Hardcoded database connection URL detected
findings.description: A database connection URL appears to be hardcoded in the source code. This is a severe data security risk as it can be extracted from version control, build artifacts, or decompiled binaries.
findings.recommendation: Move the database connection URL to a secrets manager (e.g., Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) or at minimum to environment variables. Never commit secrets to source control.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace hardcoded database connection URL with an environment variable: process.env.SECRET_NAME or inject from a secrets manager at runtime.

findings.title: Sensitive data may be logged
findings.description: Log output appears to include sensitive data fields such as passwords, tokens, or PII. This can lead to credential exposure in log aggregation systems.
findings.recommendation: Remove sensitive data from log statements. Use structured logging with redaction filters to automatically mask sensitive fields.
findings.suggestedFix: Remove sensitive fields from log calls or redact them: logger.info('User login', { userId: user.id }) instead of logging passwords/tokens.

findings.title: Weak hashing algorithm used
findings.description: MD5 or SHA1 is used, which are cryptographically broken for security purposes. They should not be used for password hashing, data integrity verification, or any security-sensitive context.
findings.recommendation: Use SHA-256/SHA-512 for integrity checks, or bcrypt/scrypt/argon2 for password hashing.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace MD5/SHA1 with SHA-256 for integrity, or bcrypt/argon2 for passwords: crypto.createHash('sha256') or await bcrypt.hash(password, 12).

findings.title: Potential SQL injection via string concatenation
findings.description:
SQL queries appear to be constructed using string interpolation or concatenation with user input, which can lead to SQL injection attacks and data breaches.

**Confirmed data flow**: `req.body` (line 64) via hashedPw → sink at line 77
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings directly.
findings.suggestedFix: Use parameterized queries: db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [userId]) instead of string concatenation.

findings.title: Cookie may lack security flags
findings.description: Cookies are set without explicit Secure, HttpOnly, or SameSite flags, making them vulnerable to interception and XSS-based theft.
findings.recommendation: Set Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite=Strict (or Lax) flags on all cookies. Use __Host- prefix for sensitive cookies.
findings.suggestedFix: Add security flags: res.cookie('name', value, { secure: true, httpOnly: true, sameSite: 'strict' });

findings.title: File upload without type/size validation
findings.description: File uploads are accepted without visible MIME type, extension, or size validation, allowing malicious file uploads.
findings.recommendation: Validate file type (MIME + extension + magic bytes), enforce size limits, scan for malware, and store uploads outside the webroot.
findings.suggestedFix: Add file validation: multer({ fileFilter, limits: { fileSize: 5 * 1024 * 1024 } }) with MIME type and extension checks.

findings.title: No CSRF protection detected
findings.description: POST endpoints exist but no CSRF tokens or protection middleware is visible, making the application vulnerable to cross-site request forgery.
findings.recommendation: Implement CSRF protection using tokens (csurf, django.middleware.csrf, @csrf_exempt annotations) or SameSite cookies.
findings.suggestedFix: Add CSRF middleware: app.use(csurf({ cookie: { httpOnly: true, sameSite: 'strict' } })); and include token in forms.

findings.title: Potential XSS via innerHTML
findings.description: Setting innerHTML, dangerouslySetInnerHTML, v-html, or [innerHTML] can lead to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) if the content includes unsanitized user input.

findings.suggestedFix: Sanitize with DOMPurify: el.innerHTML = DOMPurify.sanitize(untrustedHtml); or use textContent for plain text.

findings.title: TLS certificate validation disabled
findings.description: TLS certificate verification is explicitly disabled, making the application vulnerable to man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks.
findings.recommendation: Never disable TLS certificate validation in production. Use proper CA certificates. If using self-signed certs in development, use a CA bundle instead.

findings.title: Linter/type-checker suppression directives found

findings.suggestedFix: Add justification comments: // eslint-disable-next-line no-explicit-any -- legacy API returns untyped response, tracked in JIRA-1234.

findings.title: No password complexity validation
findings.description: Authentication endpoints handle passwords but no password complexity rules (minimum length, character requirements) are visible.
findings.recommendation: Enforce minimum 8-character passwords with complexity requirements. Use NIST SP 800-63B guidelines. Check against breached password databases (Have I Been Pwned).

findings.title: Authentication endpoints without rate limiting
findings.description: Authentication-related code exists without visible rate limiting, making it vulnerable to brute-force and credential stuffing attacks.
findings.recommendation: Implement rate limiting on login/auth endpoints. Use progressive delays, account lockouts, or CAPTCHA after failed attempts.
findings.suggestedFix: Add auth rate limiting: app.use('/login', rateLimit({ windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000, max: 5 })); lock account after 10 failures.

findings.title: Nested loops detected — potential O(n²) complexity
findings.description: Nested loops can lead to quadratic or worse time complexity. At scale, this causes dramatically increased compute costs and response times.
findings.recommendation: Consider using hash maps for lookups (O(1)), sorting + binary search, or restructuring the algorithm. If the nested loop is necessary, ensure the inner dataset is bounded.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace the inner loop with a Map/Set lookup, e.g. `const lookup = new Map(items.map(i => [i.id, i]));` then use `lookup.get(id)` instead of iterating.

findings.title: Potential N+1 query pattern (await in loop)
findings.description: An await call inside a loop suggests sequential asynchronous operations that could be batched. This causes N+1 performance problems and increased latency/cost.
findings.recommendation: Use Promise.all() to parallelize independent operations, or batch database queries (e.g., WHERE id IN (...) instead of per-ID queries).
findings.suggestedFix: Collect IDs first, then batch-fetch: `const results = await db.query('SELECT * FROM items WHERE id IN (?)', [ids]);` or use `await Promise.all(ids.map(fetchItem))`.

findings.title: Unbounded data query
findings.description: A query fetches all records without filtering or pagination. With growing data, this will consume excessive memory, bandwidth, and compute.
findings.recommendation: Add pagination (LIMIT/OFFSET or cursor-based), filtering (WHERE clauses), and projection (select only needed fields). Default to a reasonable page size.
findings.suggestedFix: Add LIMIT and WHERE clauses, e.g. `db.find({ status: 'active' }).limit(100)` or `SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE active = true LIMIT 100`.

findings.title: Synchronous/blocking file I/O detected
findings.description: Synchronous file operations block the event loop or thread, reducing throughput and wasting compute resources — especially costly in serverless environments billed per-ms.
findings.recommendation: Use asynchronous file operations (fs.promises.readFile, aiofiles, async File.ReadAllTextAsync) or streaming for large files.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace `fs.readFileSync(path)` with `await fs.promises.readFile(path)` or use streaming: `const stream = fs.createReadStream(path);`.

findings.title: Global mutable state detected
findings.description: Top-level mutable variables (let/var with object/array initialization) create shared state that prevents safe horizontal scaling across multiple instances.
findings.recommendation: Externalize state to a database, cache (Redis), or message queue. Use const/final/immutable for configuration. Each instance should be stateless.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace the top-level mutable variable with a call to an external store (e.g., `await redis.get(key)`) so each instance remains stateless.

findings.title: Synchronous blocking operation
findings.description: Blocking/synchronous operations in the request path limit concurrency and throughput. Under load, this creates a bottleneck that prevents scaling.
findings.recommendation: Use asynchronous alternatives (async/await, promises, non-blocking I/O). Move long-running work to background queues.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace the synchronous call with its async counterpart (e.g., `fs.readFileSync` → `await fs.promises.readFile`) to avoid blocking the event loop.

findings.title: External calls without timeout
findings.description: HTTP/API calls without timeouts can hang indefinitely, consuming resources and cascading failures through the system when downstream services are slow.
findings.recommendation: Set explicit timeouts on all external calls (e.g., 5-30 seconds). Implement circuit breakers (e.g., using libraries like cockatiel or opossum) for critical dependencies.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a timeout option to the HTTP call (e.g., `fetch(url, { signal: AbortSignal.timeout(5000) })`) to prevent indefinite hangs.

findings.title: CPU-intensive computation may block scaling
findings.description: Detected nested loops. Heavy computation on the main thread blocks the event loop (Node.js) or consumes thread pool capacity.
findings.recommendation: Offload CPU-intensive work to worker threads, a job queue (Bull, Celery), or a dedicated compute service. Use async variants of crypto operations (pbkdf2, scrypt). Consider WebAssembly for hot-path computation.
findings.reference: Node.js Worker Threads / Job Queue Patterns
findings.suggestedFix: Move the heavy computation into a worker thread or use the async variant (e.g., `crypto.pbkdf2` instead of `crypto.pbkdf2Sync`) to keep the main thread free.

findings.title: Image missing alt attribute
findings.description: Images must have descriptive alt text for screen readers and assistive technologies.
findings.recommendation: Add meaningful alt text describing the image content. Use alt="" only for purely decorative images.
findings.suggestedFix: Add descriptive alt text: or alt="" for decorative images.

findings.title: Click handler without keyboard equivalent
findings.description: Interactive elements with onClick must also support keyboard interaction for users who cannot use a mouse.
findings.recommendation: Add onKeyDown or onKeyPress handlers alongside onClick. Ensure all interactive elements are keyboard accessible.
findings.suggestedFix: Add keyboard support: <button onClick={handler} onKeyDown={(e) => e.key === 'Enter' && handler()}> or use native <button> elements which handle this automatically.

findings.title: Non-semantic element used with ARIA role
findings.description: Using div with an ARIA role instead of the appropriate semantic HTML element reduces accessibility and adds unnecessary complexity.
findings.recommendation: Use semantic HTML elements (button, a, h1-h6, nav, main) instead of divs with ARIA roles.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace <div role="button"> with <button>, <div role="link"> with <a href>, <div role="navigation"> with <nav>, etc.

findings.title: Form input missing label association
findings.description: Form inputs without associated labels are inaccessible to screen reader users.
findings.recommendation: Associate each input with a <label> element using for/id, or use aria-label / aria-labelledby.
findings.suggestedFix: Associate labels: <label htmlFor="email">Email</label><input id="email"> or use aria-label={"Email address"} directly on the input.

findings.title: Missing lang attribute on <html>
findings.description: The html element must have a lang attribute so screen readers pronounce content in the correct language.
findings.recommendation: Add lang attribute: <html lang="en">. Use the appropriate BCP 47 language tag.
findings.suggestedFix: Add language attribute: <html lang="en"> using the appropriate BCP 47 language tag for your content.

findings.title: Focus indicator removed (outline: none)
findings.description: Removing the focus outline without providing an alternative focus indicator makes the page unusable for keyboard users.
findings.recommendation: If removing outline, provide a visible alternative focus indicator (box-shadow, border, custom :focus-visible styles).
findings.suggestedFix: Provide alternative focus styles: :focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #4A90D9; outline-offset: 2px; } instead of outline: none.

findings.title: Form error not associated with input via ARIA
findings.description: Error messages near form inputs should be programmatically associated so screen readers announce them.
findings.recommendation: Use aria-describedby to link error messages to inputs, and aria-invalid='true' on invalid inputs.
findings.suggestedFix: Associate errors with inputs: Invalid email.

findings.title: Verb in REST endpoint URL
findings.description: REST endpoint URLs should use nouns, not verbs. The HTTP method should convey the action.
findings.recommendation: Use noun-based URLs (e.g., POST /users instead of POST /createUser). Let HTTP methods convey the action.
findings.suggestedFix: Rename the endpoint to a noun-based path (e.g., change '/createUser' to '/users') and use the appropriate HTTP method (POST) to express the action.

findings.title: SELECT * in API handler
findings.description: Returning all columns from a database query in an API response may expose sensitive data and waste bandwidth.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly select only the fields needed for the API response. Use DTOs or view models to shape the output.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace `SELECT *` with an explicit column list (e.g., `SELECT id, name, email`) and map the result through a DTO before returning it.

findings.title: List endpoint without pagination
findings.description: API endpoints that return collections should support pagination to prevent unbounded responses.
findings.recommendation: Implement pagination using limit/offset, cursor-based, or page-based approaches. Include total count and navigation links.
findings.suggestedFix: Accept `page` and `limit` query parameters, apply them to the database query (e.g., `.skip().limit()`), and return `{ data, total, page }` in the response.

findings.title: No API versioning detected
findings.description: APIs should be versioned to allow backward-compatible evolution.
findings.recommendation: Add API versioning via URL path (/v1/resource), header (X-API-Version), or query parameter.
findings.suggestedFix: Prefix all route paths with a version segment (e.g., `/v1/users`) or mount the router under a `/v1` base path.

findings.title: Inconsistent API response structure
findings.description: Some responses use a wrapper (e.g., { data: ... }) while others return raw data. This inconsistency complicates client consumption.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a consistent response envelope (e.g., { data, meta, errors }) across all endpoints.
findings.suggestedFix: Wrap all successful responses in a standard envelope (e.g., `res.json({ data: result })`) and error responses in `{ error: { message, code } }`.

findings.title: Request body used without content-type validation
findings.description: Consuming request bodies without verifying Content-Type can lead to parsing errors or security issues.
findings.recommendation: Use body-parsing middleware (express.json()) and validate Content-Type headers. Reject requests with unexpected content types.
findings.suggestedFix: Add `app.use(express.json())` and a middleware that returns 415 Unsupported Media Type when the Content-Type header is not `application/json`.

findings.title: Sensitive data in URL/query parameters
findings.description: Passwords, tokens, and secrets in URL paths or query strings are logged in server access logs, browser history, and proxy caches.
findings.recommendation: Pass sensitive data in request headers (Authorization) or request body, never in URLs or query parameters.
findings.suggestedFix: Move the sensitive value from the URL/query string into the `Authorization` header or the request body and update the route accordingly.

findings.title: Network call without timeout
findings.description: Network calls without timeouts can hang indefinitely, causing resource exhaustion and cascading failures.
findings.recommendation: Set explicit timeouts on all network calls. Use AbortController with setTimeout for fetch, or timeout options for HTTP clients.
findings.suggestedFix: Add timeout: const controller = new AbortController(); setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), 5000); fetch(url, { signal: controller.signal });

findings.title: Abrupt process termination detected
findings.description: Calling process.exit(), panic!(), System.exit(), or os.Exit() prevents graceful shutdown, skips cleanup handlers, and can cause data loss.
findings.recommendation: Throw errors or use graceful shutdown patterns instead. Let the process exit naturally after cleanup. Reserve panics for truly unrecoverable situations.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace process.exit() with graceful shutdown: process.on('SIGTERM', async () => { await server.close(); await db.disconnect(); });

findings.title: No fallback for failed external call
findings.description: External calls catch errors but don't provide fallback values or degraded functionality.
findings.recommendation: Provide fallback behavior: cached responses, default values, or gracefully degraded features when dependencies fail.
findings.suggestedFix: Add fallback: try { data = await fetchFromApi(); } catch { data = await cache.get(key) ?? DEFAULT_VALUE; logger.warn('Using fallback data'); }

findings.title: Write endpoints without idempotency support
findings.description: POST/PUT endpoints without idempotency keys can cause duplicate operations when clients retry after network failures.
findings.recommendation: Accept an idempotency key header (Idempotency-Key) and use it to deduplicate write operations.
findings.suggestedFix: Add idempotency: const key = req.headers['idempotency-key']; if (key && await cache.has(key)) return res.json(await cache.get(key)); // process then cache result.

findings.title: Potential N+1 query pattern
findings.description: Database queries inside loops cause N+1 performance problems, generating excessive database load that grows linearly with data size.
findings.recommendation: Batch queries outside the loop using WHERE IN clauses, JOINs, or DataLoader patterns. Fetch all needed data in a single query.
findings.suggestedFix: Batch queries outside the loop: const items = await db.findMany({ where: { id: { in: ids } } }); then iterate over the result.

findings.title: Synchronous / blocking I/O detected
findings.description: Synchronous file or blocking operations can block the event loop (Node.js), thread, or async runtime, degrading throughput under concurrent load.
findings.recommendation: Use async/await versions, non-blocking APIs, or spawn blocking work on a separate thread/runtime. Sync I/O is only acceptable at startup.
findings.suggestedFix: Use async variants: await fs.promises.readFile() instead of fs.readFileSync(), or asyncio.open() in Python.

findings.title: Duplicate fetch calls to same URL
findings.description: Multiple requests to the same URL within the same module suggest missing caching or request deduplication.
findings.recommendation: Cache responses or deduplicate requests. Use memoization, request coalescing, or an in-memory/distributed cache.
findings.suggestedFix: Cache or deduplicate: const cached = cache.get(url) ?? await fetch(url); cache.set(url, cached); or use a request deduplication layer.

findings.title: Heavy library imported eagerly
findings.description: Large libraries are imported at the top level, which increases initial bundle size and load time.
findings.recommendation: Use dynamic imports (import()) for heavy libraries. Import specific sub-modules (lodash/get instead of lodash). Consider tree-shakeable alternatives.
findings.suggestedFix: Use dynamic imports: const lodash = await import('lodash/get'); or import specific sub-modules: import get from 'lodash/get'.

findings.title: DOM manipulation inside loop
findings.description: Modifying the DOM inside a loop causes repeated layout recalculations (reflows), severely degrading rendering performance.
findings.recommendation: Build DOM content in a DocumentFragment or string, then insert once. Use virtual DOM frameworks or batch updates.
findings.suggestedFix: Batch DOM updates: const fragment = document.createDocumentFragment(); items.forEach(item => fragment.appendChild(el)); container.appendChild(fragment);

findings.title: Unbounded data fetch without pagination
findings.description: Fetching all records without limit/pagination can exhaust memory and crash the application as data grows.
findings.recommendation: Always use pagination (limit/offset or cursor-based) when querying collections. Set reasonable default page sizes.
findings.suggestedFix: Add pagination: db.find({}).limit(50).skip(page * 50) or use cursor-based pagination for large datasets.

findings.title: Nested loops detected — O(n²) or worse complexity
findings.description: 1 nested loop(s) detected. Nested loops scale quadratically or worse with input size and can cause severe performance degradation on large datasets.
findings.recommendation: Replace nested loops with hash maps (O(n)) for lookups, pre-sorted data with binary search, or purpose-built data structures. Consider if the algorithm can be flattened.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace nested loop with Map lookup: const map = new Map(items.map(x => [x.id, x])); for (const item of others) { const match = map.get(item.id); }

findings.title: setInterval without clearInterval — timer leak
findings.description: setInterval is called without a corresponding clearInterval, which creates a timer that runs indefinitely and prevents garbage collection of captured closures.
findings.recommendation: Store the interval ID and call clearInterval in cleanup/teardown logic (e.g., useEffect cleanup, componentWillUnmount, or process exit handler).
findings.suggestedFix: Store and clear: const id = setInterval(fn, delay); // in cleanup: clearInterval(id); or use useEffect: useEffect(() => { const id = setInterval(...); return () => clearInterval(id); }, []);

findings.title: Shared mutable state in async context
findings.description: Module-level mutable variables accessed from async functions can cause race conditions and data corruption.
findings.recommendation: Use request-scoped/context-scoped state, atomic operations, or proper synchronization mechanisms instead of shared mutable variables.
findings.suggestedFix: Move the mutable variable into a request-scoped context (e.g., pass it as a function parameter or store it in `AsyncLocalStorage`) instead of using a module-level `let`.

findings.title: Potentially missing await on async operation
findings.description: Async operations without await fire-and-forget, meaning errors are silently lost and operations may not complete before the response is sent.
findings.recommendation: Add await to async operations, or explicitly handle the returned promise with .catch(). Use ESLint's no-floating-promises rule.
findings.suggestedFix: Prepend `await` to the async call (e.g., `await db.save(entity)`) and enable the `@typescript-eslint/no-floating-promises` lint rule to catch future occurrences.

findings.title: Sequential await in loop
findings.description: Using await inside a loop processes items sequentially. If operations are independent, this unnecessarily serializes them.
findings.recommendation: For independent operations, collect promises and use Promise.all() (with concurrency limits). Keep sequential only if order matters.
findings.suggestedFix: Refactor the loop body to build an array of promises and resolve them with `await Promise.all(promises)` (or `pMap` for bounded concurrency) outside the loop.

findings.title: setInterval without clearInterval
findings.description: Intervals without cleanup continue running after the component/module is no longer needed, causing memory leaks and unexpected behavior.
findings.recommendation: Store the interval ID and call clearInterval in cleanup/unmount/dispose handlers.
findings.suggestedFix: Assign the return value to a variable (`const intervalId = setInterval(...)`) and call `clearInterval(intervalId)` in the component's cleanup or `process.on('SIGTERM')` handler.

findings.title: Hardcoded credentials in source code
findings.description: Found 4 instance(s) of what appears to be hardcoded credentials. Credentials in source code are exposed in version control and cannot be rotated without redeployment.
findings.recommendation: Use environment variables or a secrets manager (Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit credentials to version control.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace hardcoded credentials with environment variables: process.env.SECRET_NAME (Node.js), os.environ['SECRET_NAME'] (Python), or inject from a secrets manager.

findings.title: Sensitive tokens passed in query parameters
findings.description: Authentication tokens or API keys are read from query parameters. Query params appear in server logs, browser history, referrer headers, and proxy logs.
findings.recommendation: Pass tokens in the Authorization header (Bearer scheme) or in httpOnly cookies. Never use query parameters for sensitive credentials.
findings.suggestedFix: Read tokens from the Authorization header instead: const token = req.headers.authorization?.replace('Bearer ', '');

findings.title: Weak hashing algorithm for credentials
findings.description: MD5, SHA1, or SHA256 are fast hash algorithms unsuitable for password storage. They can be brute-forced at billions of hashes per second.
findings.recommendation: Use bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 for password hashing. These algorithms are intentionally slow and include salt by default.
findings.reference: OWASP Password Storage Cheat Sheet / NIST 800-63b
findings.suggestedFix: Replace with bcrypt/argon2: bcrypt.hash(password, 12) (JS), bcrypt.hashpw(password, bcrypt.gensalt()) (Python), Argon2::default().hash_password() (Rust), BCrypt.HashPassword() (C#).

findings.title: TLS certificate validation disabled
findings.description: TLS certificate verification is disabled, allowing man-in-the-middle attacks. Authentication credentials sent over this connection can be intercepted.
findings.recommendation: Never disable TLS verification in production. Fix certificate issues properly. Use CA bundles for self-signed certs in development only.
findings.suggestedFix: Remove TLS bypass: delete rejectUnauthorized:false (JS), verify=False (Python), InsecureSkipVerify:true (Go), danger_accept_invalid_certs(true) (Rust), TrustAllCerts (Java).

findings.title: Cookies set without Secure/HttpOnly flags
findings.description: Cookies are set without Secure (HTTPS-only) or HttpOnly (no JS access) flags. This exposes cookies to interception and XSS-based theft.
findings.recommendation: Set cookies with { secure: true, httpOnly: true, sameSite: 'strict' }. Use Secure for all auth cookies. HttpOnly prevents JavaScript access.
findings.reference: OWASP Secure Cookie Best Practices / CWE-614
findings.suggestedFix: Add security flags: res.cookie('session', token, { httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'strict' });

findings.title: SQL injection via string concatenation
findings.description:
Found 1 instance(s) of SQL queries built with string concatenation or interpolation containing user input. This is the most common and dangerous database vulnerability.

**Confirmed data flow**: `req.body` (line 64) via hashedPw → sink at line 77
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries (placeholders) or prepared statements. ORMs handle this automatically. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.
findings.reference: OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet / CWE-89
findings.suggestedFix: Use parameterized queries: db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [userId]) (JS), cursor.execute('...WHERE id = %s', (uid,)) (Python), db.Query('...WHERE id = $1', id) (Go).

findings.title: SELECT * retrieves unnecessary columns
findings.description: Found 1 SELECT * query/queries. Selecting all columns transfers unnecessary data, breaks when schema changes, and prevents index-only scans.
findings.recommendation: Select only the columns you need: SELECT id, name, email FROM users. This reduces network transfer, memory usage, and improves query plan optimization.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace SELECT * with explicit columns: SELECT id, name, email FROM users WHERE active = true; — reduces data transfer and enables index-only scans.

findings.title: N+1 query pattern detected
findings.description: Found 9 database query/queries inside loops. This creates N+1 queries: 1 for the list + N for each item. Performance degrades linearly with data volume.
findings.recommendation: Use batch queries (WHERE id IN (...)), JOINs, or ORM eager loading (include/populate) to fetch related data in a single query.
findings.reference: N+1 Query Problem / ORM Performance Patterns
findings.suggestedFix: Batch queries: const items = await db.query('SELECT * FROM items WHERE parent_id = ANY($1)', [parentIds]); instead of querying in a loop.

findings.title: Data mutations without transaction handling
findings.description: Data is modified (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) without transaction wrappers. If an error occurs mid-operation, data could be left in an inconsistent state.
findings.recommendation: Wrap multi-step data mutations in transactions. Use BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK or ORM transaction APIs to ensure atomicity.
findings.reference: ACID Properties / Database Transaction Best Practices
findings.suggestedFix: Wrap mutations in transaction: await db.transaction(async (trx) => { await trx('orders').insert(order); await trx('inventory').decrement('qty', 1); });

findings.title: Hardcoded database connection string
findings.description: Database connection string is hardcoded in source code, exposing credentials and making it impossible to use different databases per environment.
findings.recommendation: Use environment variables for connection strings. Store credentials in a secrets manager. Use different connection strings per environment.
findings.suggestedFix: Use env vars: const connectionString = process.env.DATABASE_URL; never hardcode credentials in source code.

findings.title: Destructive DDL statements in application code
findings.description: Found 1 DROP/TRUNCATE statement(s). These permanently delete data or schema. If executed accidentally (e.g., via injection), data loss is irreversible.
findings.recommendation: Never run destructive DDL from application code. Use migration tools (Prisma, Flyway, Alembic) with review and rollback support. Require elevated permissions for DDL.
findings.reference: Database Migration Best Practices / Least Privilege
findings.suggestedFix: Move DDL to migration files: npx prisma migrate dev --name drop_legacy_table; never embed DROP TABLE in application code.

findings.title: Database credentials embedded in connection string
findings.description: Connection string contains inline username and password. These credentials are visible in source code, logs, and process listings.
findings.recommendation: Use separate credential parameters or environment variables. Consider IAM/managed identity for passwordless database connections in cloud environments.
findings.reference: OWASP: Credential Management / Azure Managed Identity
findings.suggestedFix: Use env vars: const client = new Client({ host: process.env.DB_HOST, user: process.env.DB_USER, password: process.env.DB_PASSWORD });

findings.title: OS-specific environment variables used directly
findings.description: Found 1 reference(s) to platform-specific environment variables (e.g., APPDATA, USERPROFILE, XDG_*). These variables only exist on specific operating systems.
findings.recommendation: Use cross-platform helpers like os.homedir(), os.tmpdir(), or libraries like 'env-paths' to resolve platform-appropriate directories.
findings.reference: Node.js os Module / Cross-Platform File Paths
findings.suggestedFix: Replace `process.env.APPDATA` with `os.homedir()` or use the `env-paths` package to get platform-appropriate config/data directories.

findings.title: Browser-specific APIs used in server-side code
findings.description: Found 2 browser-only API call(s) (document, window, localStorage, etc.) in what appears to be server-side code. These will throw ReferenceError at runtime.
findings.recommendation: Guard browser API usage with typeof checks (e.g., typeof window !== 'undefined'). Use isomorphic libraries for code shared between client and server.
findings.suggestedFix: Wrap browser API calls in a guard: `if (typeof window !== 'undefined') { window.localStorage.setItem(...) }` or move them to a client-only module.

evaluations.summary:
**Judge UX** — User Experience & Interface Quality
Verdict: **WARNING** | Score: **69/100**
Findings: 0 critical, 0 high, 3 medium, 3 low

Key issues:

findings.title: Inline event handlers in HTML
findings.description: Found 1 inline event handler(s). Inline handlers mix behavior with markup, break CSP policies, and are harder to maintain.
findings.recommendation: Use addEventListener() or framework event bindings (React onClick, Vue @click). Separate behavior from markup for maintainability and CSP compliance.
findings.reference: MDN: Inline Event Handlers / Content Security Policy
findings.suggestedFix: Remove the inline `on*=` attribute and attach the handler in JavaScript via `element.addEventListener('click', handler)` or the framework equivalent.

findings.title: Form submission without loading/disabled state
findings.description: Forms are submitted without visible loading state or button disabling. Users may click multiple times causing duplicate submissions.
findings.recommendation: Disable the submit button during submission. Show a loading indicator. Prevent double-submission at the application layer.
findings.reference: Nielsen's Heuristic #1: Visibility of System Status
findings.suggestedFix: Add an `isSubmitting` state flag that disables the submit button and shows a spinner while the form request is in flight.

findings.title: Destructive actions without confirmation
findings.description: Destructive operations (delete, remove) are handled without confirmation prompts. Users could accidentally destroy data.
findings.recommendation: Add confirmation dialogs for destructive actions. Show what will be affected. Consider soft-delete with undo capability.
findings.suggestedFix: Guard the delete handler with a confirmation prompt, e.g., `if (!confirm('Are you sure you want to delete this item?')) return;`.

findings.title: List endpoints without pagination
findings.description: Data retrieval endpoints return all results without pagination. This causes slow responses, high memory usage, and poor UX with large datasets.
findings.recommendation: Implement pagination (offset-based or cursor-based). Return total count and page info. Enforce maximum page sizes.
findings.suggestedFix: Accept `page` and `limit` query parameters (e.g., `const { page = 1, limit = 20 } = req.query`) and apply `.skip((page-1)*limit).limit(limit)` to the query.

findings.title: Mutations without success feedback
findings.description: POST/PUT/DELETE operations found without visible success feedback. Users don't know if their action worked, leading to repeated submissions.
findings.recommendation: Show success notifications (toasts, alerts) after mutations. Provide clear visual feedback. Consider optimistic UI updates with rollback on failure.
findings.reference: Nielsen's Heuristic #1: Visibility of System Status
findings.suggestedFix: Add a `toast.success('Changes saved successfully')` call (or equivalent notification) in the `.then()` or after the `await` of the mutation request.

findings.title: Form submission without client-side validation
findings.description: Found 1 form submission handler(s) without visible validation. Submitting invalid data wastes round trips and frustrates users with server-side error messages.
findings.recommendation: Add client-side validation before submission. Use schema validation libraries (Zod, Yup, Joi). Show inline validation feedback. Keep server-side validation as well.
findings.reference: UX: Form Validation Patterns / Nielsen's Heuristic #9: Error Recovery
findings.suggestedFix: Add a validation check at the top of the submit handler (e.g., `const result = schema.safeParse(formData); if (!result.success) return showErrors(result.error)`) before sending the request.

evaluations.summary:
**Judge Logging Privacy** — Logging Privacy & Data Redaction
Verdict: **FAIL** | Score: **20/100**
Findings: 2 critical, 0 high, 3 medium, 1 low

Key issues:
- [LOGPRIV-001] (critical) Authentication tokens logged: Found 1 instance(s) where authentication tokens or authorization headers are logged. Tokens in logs can be used for session hijacking.
- [LOGPRIV-002] (critical) Passwords/secrets logged: Found 1 instance(s) where passwords or secrets appear in log statements. This exposes credentials in log files, monitoring systems, and SIEM tools.

findings.title: Authentication tokens logged
findings.description: Found 1 instance(s) where authentication tokens or authorization headers are logged. Tokens in logs can be used for session hijacking.
findings.recommendation: Never log authentication tokens, Authorization headers, or session IDs. If request logging is needed, redact sensitive headers before logging.
findings.suggestedFix: Redact auth headers: const safeHeaders = { ...req.headers, authorization: '[REDACTED]' }; logger.info({ headers: safeHeaders });

findings.title: Passwords/secrets logged
findings.description: Found 1 instance(s) where passwords or secrets appear in log statements. This exposes credentials in log files, monitoring systems, and SIEM tools.
findings.recommendation: Never log passwords, credentials, or secrets. Implement a log sanitizer that redacts sensitive fields automatically.
findings.reference: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet / GDPR Art. 5(1)(f)
findings.suggestedFix: Remove password from log output: const { password, ...safeData } = userData; logger.info({ user: safeData });

findings.title: Unstructured logging lacks redaction capabilities
findings.description: Found 12 unstructured log statement(s). Console/print logging has no built-in redaction, log level filtering, or structured output — making it impossible to automatically strip sensitive data.
findings.recommendation: Use a structured logging library (pino, winston) that supports field-level redaction, log level filtering, and structured output for automated sensitivity scanning.
findings.suggestedFix: Use pino with redaction: import pino from 'pino'; const logger = pino({ redact: ['req.headers.authorization', '*.password', '*.ssn'] });

findings.title: String concatenation in log statements
findings.description: Found 5 log statement(s) using string concatenation. Concatenated logs are unstructured and make it impossible to apply field-level redaction.
findings.recommendation: Use structured logging with named fields: logger.info({ userId, action }, 'User action performed'). This allows automated redaction of specific fields.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace concatenation with structured fields: instead of console.log('User ' + id + ' did ' + action), use logger.info({ userId: id, action }, 'User action performed');

findings.title: IP addresses logged without anonymization
findings.description: Found 1 instance(s) where IP addresses are logged. Under GDPR, IP addresses are personal data and must be handled accordingly.
findings.recommendation: Anonymize IP addresses in logs (truncate last octet for IPv4, mask prefix for IPv6). If full IP is needed for security, ensure log retention complies with privacy policy.
findings.reference: GDPR Recital 30: IP Addresses as Personal Data
findings.suggestedFix: Anonymize IPs: function anonymizeIp(ip) { return ip.replace(/\d+$/, '0'); } logger.info({ ip: anonymizeIp(req.ip) });

findings.title: Database queries logged — may contain sensitive parameters
findings.description: Found 1 instance(s) where SQL queries are logged. Query parameters often contain user data (emails, names, IDs) that shouldn't appear in logs.
findings.recommendation: Log query templates without parameter values. Use parameterized query logging that replaces bind values with placeholders. Redact sensitive column values.
findings.reference: Database Logging Privacy / OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
findings.suggestedFix: Log queries safely: logger.info({ query: 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', paramCount: params.length }); // never log actual parameter values.

evaluations.summary:
**Judge Rate Limiting** — Rate Limiting & Throttling
Verdict: **WARNING** | Score: **61/100**
Findings: 0 critical, 2 high, 1 medium, 1 low

Key issues:
- [RATE-003] (high) Authentication endpoints without rate limiting: Found 1 authentication endpoint(s) without visible rate limiting. Auth endpoints are prime targets for brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks.
- [RATE-004] (high) File upload without size or count limits: Found 2 file upload handler(s) without visible size limits. Unbounded uploads can exhaust disk space and memory, causing denial of service.

findings.title: Unbounded query results without limit
findings.description: Found 1 database query/queries without a limit. A single request could return millions of rows, crashing the server.
findings.recommendation: Always enforce a maximum result limit: db.find({}).limit(100). Implement pagination and enforce maximum page sizes.
findings.reference: API Rate Limiting / Database Query Safety
findings.suggestedFix: Add query limits: db.find({}).limit(100).skip(page * 100); enforce max page size: const limit = Math.min(req.query.limit || 20, 100);

findings.title: setInterval without rate control
findings.description: setInterval runs indefinitely and could generate excessive load. If the interval function is slow, executions can overlap and compound.
findings.recommendation: Use setTimeout with re-scheduling instead of setInterval to prevent overlap. Add guards to skip execution if the previous run hasn't completed.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace setInterval with controlled scheduling: async function poll() { await doWork(); setTimeout(poll, interval); } poll(); — prevents overlapping executions.

findings.title: Authentication endpoints without rate limiting
findings.description: Found 1 authentication endpoint(s) without visible rate limiting. Auth endpoints are prime targets for brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks.
findings.recommendation: Apply strict rate limits to auth endpoints (e.g., 5-10 requests/minute per IP). Use progressive delays or CAPTCHA after failed attempts. Consider using 'express-rate-limit' or 'rate-limiter-flexible'.
findings.suggestedFix: Add auth rate limit: app.use('/auth', rateLimit({ windowMs: 60000, max: 5 })); from 'express-rate-limit'.

findings.title: File upload without size or count limits
findings.description: Found 2 file upload handler(s) without visible size limits. Unbounded uploads can exhaust disk space and memory, causing denial of service.
findings.recommendation: Set explicit file size limits (e.g., multer({ limits: { fileSize: 5 * 1024 * 1024 } })). Limit the number of files per request. Validate file types.
findings.reference: OWASP: Unrestricted File Upload / Multer Limits
findings.suggestedFix: Set upload limits: const upload = multer({ limits: { fileSize: 5 * 1024 * 1024, files: 5 }, fileFilter: allowedTypes });

findings.title: Placeholder security comment — missing implementation
findings.description: Found 1 TODO/FIXME comment(s) indicating that security-critical functionality (authentication, validation, encryption, etc.) has not been implemented yet. AI-generated code often leaves these placeholders which are easy to overlook during review.
findings.recommendation: Implement the security controls indicated by each comment before merging. If the control is not needed, remove the comment and document why. Do not ship TODO security comments to production.
findings.reference: CWE-1188: Insecure Default Initialization of Resource
findings.suggestedFix: Replace each TODO/FIXME security comment with a working implementation or remove the comment and document why the control is unnecessary.

findings.title: Type safety bypassed in security-critical code
findings.description: TypeScript 'as any' or untyped 'any' usage found near authentication, cryptographic, or validation code. Bypassing the type system in security-sensitive areas can hide type mismatches that lead to vulnerabilities.
findings.recommendation: Define proper interfaces for security-related data structures (tokens, sessions, credentials). Replace 'as any' with explicit types or runtime validation (zod, io-ts).
findings.reference: CWE-704: Incorrect Type Conversion or Cast
findings.suggestedFix: Replace 'as any' with a proper interface: interface TokenPayload { sub: string; exp: number; roles: string[] } and validate at runtime with zod or io-ts.

findings.title: Hardcoded URLs or IP addresses
findings.description: Found 2 hardcoded URL(s) or IP address(es). AI-generated code frequently hardcodes endpoints that should be configurable per environment. Hardcoded production URLs in source code can leak internal infrastructure details.
findings.recommendation: Move all endpoint URLs and IP addresses to environment variables or a configuration file. Use service discovery or DNS for internal services.
findings.suggestedFix: Move URLs to environment variables: const apiUrl = process.env.API_URL ?? 'http://localhost:3000'; and load from .env files per environment.

findings.title: Request handlers without input validation
findings.description: Found 6 API endpoint handler(s) but no input validation library or schema validation is detected. AI-generated API code frequently omits input validation, leaving endpoints vulnerable to injection, type confusion, and data integrity issues.
findings.recommendation: Add schema validation for all request inputs (body, query, params). Use zod, joi, or yup (Node.js), pydantic (Python), class-validator (NestJS), or equivalent for your framework. Validate at the boundary before any business logic.
findings.reference: OWASP Input Validation — CWE-20: Improper Input Validation
findings.suggestedFix: Add schema validation at the boundary: app.post('/api', validate(schema), handler). Use zod, joi, or express-validator to validate body, query, and params.

findings.title: Weak cryptographic hash (MD5/SHA-1)
findings.description: AI-generated code frequently uses MD5 or SHA-1 for hashing. Both algorithms have known collision vulnerabilities and are unsuitable for password hashing, integrity verification, or digital signatures.
findings.recommendation: Replace MD5/SHA-1 with SHA-256+ for integrity checks, or bcrypt/scrypt/argon2 for password hashing. Use crypto.subtle.digest('SHA-256', data) in web contexts.
findings.reference: CWE-328: Use of Weak Hash — NIST SP 800-131A
findings.suggestedFix: Replace weak hashes: crypto.createHash('sha256') instead of md5/sha1. For passwords, use bcrypt: await bcrypt.hash(password, 12).

findings.title: TLS certificate verification disabled
findings.description: SSL/TLS certificate verification has been disabled (e.g. rejectUnauthorized: false, verify=False, InsecureSkipVerify: true). AI-generated code often disables certificate checks to bypass development SSL errors, leaving the code vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks in production.
findings.recommendation: Never disable TLS verification in production. Use properly signed certificates (Let's Encrypt is free). If self-signed certificates are required for internal services, configure the specific CA certificate rather than disabling all verification.
findings.reference: CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation — OWASP A07:2021
findings.suggestedFix: Remove rejectUnauthorized: false. Use proper TLS certificates or configure a custom CA: const agent = new https.Agent({ ca: fs.readFileSync('internal-ca.pem') }).

findings.title: SQL injection via string concatenation
findings.description:
Found 1 instance(s) of SQL queries built with string concatenation or interpolation containing user input. This is the most common and dangerous database vulnerability.

**Confirmed data flow**: `req.body` (line 64) via hashedPw → sink at line 77

_Also identified by: DATA-007_
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries (placeholders) or prepared statements. ORMs handle this automatically. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.
findings.reference: OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet / CWE-89
findings.suggestedFix: Use parameterized queries: db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [userId]) (JS), cursor.execute('...WHERE id = %s', (uid,)) (Python), db.Query('...WHERE id = $1', id) (Go).

findings.title: Dangerous eval()/exec() usage
findings.description:
eval(), exec(), or dynamic code compilation executes arbitrary code and is a primary vector for code injection attacks.

**Confirmed data flow**: `req.body` (line 115) → sink at line 115
findings.recommendation: Remove eval() entirely. Use JSON.parse() for data parsing (JS/TS), ast.literal_eval (Python), or a proper expression parser.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace eval(expr) with JSON.parse(expr) or a safe parser.

patch.newText: new Function(req.body.filterExpression)() /* TODO: eliminate dynamic code execution */

findings.title: Secrets hardcoded in source code
findings.description:
Found 6 instance(s) of hardcoded secrets. Secrets in code are exposed in version control, CI logs, and error traces. They cannot be rotated without redeployment.

_Also identified by: DATA-003, DATA-001, DATA-002, AUTH-001, DB-007_
findings.recommendation: Store secrets in a secrets manager (Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Inject via environment variables at runtime. Never commit secrets.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace with environment variable: process.env.SECRET_NAME || throw new Error('Missing SECRET_NAME');

findings.title: Destructive DDL statements in application code
findings.description: Found 1 DROP/TRUNCATE statement(s). These permanently delete data or schema. If executed accidentally (e.g., via injection), data loss is irreversible.
findings.recommendation: Never run destructive DDL from application code. Use migration tools (Prisma, Flyway, Alembic) with review and rollback support. Require elevated permissions for DDL.
findings.reference: Database Migration Best Practices / Least Privilege
findings.suggestedFix: Move DDL to migration files: npx prisma migrate dev --name drop_legacy_table; never embed DROP TABLE in application code.

findings.title: Sensitive data in URL/query parameters
findings.description: Passwords, tokens, and secrets in URL paths or query strings are logged in server access logs, browser history, and proxy caches.
findings.recommendation: Pass sensitive data in request headers (Authorization) or request body, never in URLs or query parameters.
findings.suggestedFix: Move the sensitive value from the URL/query string into the `Authorization` header or the request body and update the route accordingly.

findings.title: Hardcoded connection string / service URL
findings.description: Connection strings hardcoded in source code will break across environments and expose credentials in version control.
findings.recommendation: Use environment variables or a secrets manager (Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) for all connection strings.
findings.suggestedFix: Move the connection string to an environment variable or secret store and reference it via `process.env.DATABASE_URL`.

findings.title: TLS certificate verification disabled
findings.description:
SSL/TLS certificate verification has been disabled (e.g. rejectUnauthorized: false, verify=False, InsecureSkipVerify: true). AI-generated code often disables certificate checks to bypass development SSL errors, leaving the code vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks in production.

_Also identified by: AUTH-004, CYBER-003_
findings.recommendation: Never disable TLS verification in production. Use properly signed certificates (Let's Encrypt is free). If self-signed certificates are required for internal services, configure the specific CA certificate rather than disabling all verification.
findings.reference: CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation — OWASP A07:2021
findings.suggestedFix: Remove rejectUnauthorized: false. Use proper TLS certificates or configure a custom CA: const agent = new https.Agent({ ca: fs.readFileSync('internal-ca.pem') }).

findings.title: Hardcoded database connection URL detected
findings.description: A database connection URL appears to be hardcoded in the source code. This is a severe data security risk as it can be extracted from version control, build artifacts, or decompiled binaries.
findings.recommendation: Move the database connection URL to a secrets manager (e.g., Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) or at minimum to environment variables. Never commit secrets to source control.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace hardcoded database connection URL with an environment variable: process.env.SECRET_NAME or inject from a secrets manager at runtime.

findings.title: Weak hashing algorithm for credentials
findings.description:
MD5, SHA1, or SHA256 are fast hash algorithms unsuitable for password storage. They can be brute-forced at billions of hashes per second.

_Also identified by: AICS-017, DATA-006_
findings.recommendation: Use bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 for password hashing. These algorithms are intentionally slow and include salt by default.
findings.reference: OWASP Password Storage Cheat Sheet / NIST 800-63b
findings.suggestedFix: Replace with bcrypt/argon2: bcrypt.hash(password, 12) (JS), bcrypt.hashpw(password, bcrypt.gensalt()) (Python), Argon2::default().hash_password() (Rust), BCrypt.HashPassword() (C#).

findings.title: Hardcoded database connection string
findings.description: Database connection string is hardcoded in source code, exposing credentials and making it impossible to use different databases per environment.
findings.recommendation: Use environment variables for connection strings. Store credentials in a secrets manager. Use different connection strings per environment.

findings.title: Authentication tokens logged
findings.description: Found 1 instance(s) where authentication tokens or authorization headers are logged. Tokens in logs can be used for session hijacking.
findings.recommendation: Never log authentication tokens, Authorization headers, or session IDs. If request logging is needed, redact sensitive headers before logging.

findings.title: Sensitive data in log statements

findings.recommendation: Never log sensitive data. Use redaction/masking utilities to sanitize log output. Audit all log statements.

findings.title: Potential N+1 query pattern
findings.description:
Database queries inside loops cause N+1 performance problems, generating excessive database load that grows linearly with data size.

_Also identified by: COST-002, DB-003_
findings.recommendation: Batch queries outside the loop using WHERE IN clauses, JOINs, or DataLoader patterns. Fetch all needed data in a single query.
findings.suggestedFix: Batch queries outside the loop: const items = await db.findMany({ where: { id: { in: ids } } }); then iterate over the result.

findings.title: PII field handled without protection
findings.description:
Personally Identifiable Information (SSN, passport, tax ID) must be encrypted at rest and in transit, and masked in logs.

_Also identified by: SOV-003_
findings.recommendation: Encrypt PII fields, mask them in logs and UI displays, and ensure they are stored with column-level encryption.
findings.suggestedFix: Encrypt PII at rest: const encrypted = crypto.createCipheriv('aes-256-gcm', key, iv).update(piiValue, 'utf8', 'hex'); store only the ciphertext.

findings.title: Sensitive data potentially logged
findings.description: Log statements appear to include sensitive fields (password, token, API key, SSN, credit card). This violates security and compliance requirements.
findings.recommendation: Never log sensitive data. Use redaction middleware or mask sensitive fields before logging. Audit all log statements for PII/secrets.
findings.reference: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet / PCI DSS Requirement 3
findings.suggestedFix: Remove sensitive fields from log calls and configure redaction paths in your logger: e.g., pino({ redact: ['req.headers.authorization', 'password'] }).

findings.title: Credit card data handling detected
findings.description: Credit card numbers must never be stored in plain text. PCI DSS requires tokenization, encryption, or use of a payment processor.
findings.recommendation: Use a PCI-compliant payment processor (Stripe, Braintree). Never store, log, or transmit raw card numbers. Tokenize immediately.
findings.reference: PCI DSS Requirement 3: Protect Stored Cardholder Data
findings.suggestedFix: Replace raw card handling with tokenization: const { id } = await stripe.tokens.create({ card }); store only the token, never the card number.

findings.title: Demographic-based conditional logic
findings.description: Code contains conditional logic based on protected demographic characteristics, which may constitute discriminatory behavior.
findings.recommendation: Review whether demographic-based logic is legally compliant and ethically justified. Document the business justification. Consider bias testing.
findings.reference: EU AI Act / Anti-Discrimination Laws / Algorithmic Fairness
findings.suggestedFix: Replace demographic conditionals with policy-driven rules or feature flags, and add bias-impact documentation for any remaining demographic logic.

findings.title: Abrupt process termination instead of proper error handling
findings.description:
Found 1 abrupt termination call(s) (process.exit, sys.exit, panic, .unwrap). These skip cleanup handlers, drop in-flight requests, and can corrupt data.

_Also identified by: REL-003, CICD-001_
findings.recommendation: Use proper error propagation instead of abrupt termination. Return error responses in HTTP servers. Let the process shutdown gracefully.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace abrupt exits with graceful shutdown: server.close(() => cleanup()) (JS), raise SystemExit (Python), return Err(...) instead of .unwrap() (Rust), os.Exit only in main() (Go).

findings.title: Synchronous blocking operation
findings.description:
Blocking/synchronous operations in the request path limit concurrency and throughput. Under load, this creates a bottleneck that prevents scaling.

_Also identified by: PERF-002, COST-004_
findings.recommendation: Use asynchronous alternatives (async/await, promises, non-blocking I/O). Move long-running work to background queues.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace the synchronous call with its async counterpart (e.g., `fs.readFileSync` → `await fs.promises.readFile`) to avoid blocking the event loop.
